highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.119 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release makes `/config` settings like theme, editor mode, and verbose output persistent across sessions by saving them to `~/.claude/settings.json`. A new `prUrlTemplate` setting lets teams point the PR badge footer to a custom code-review URL instead of GitHub. This is a great quality-of-life update for developers who want their preferences to stick between restarts.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.118 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds Vim visual mode (`v`) and visual-line mode (`V`) support, making Claude Code much more powerful for Vim users who rely on selection-based editing. The `/cost` and `/stats` commands have been merged into a unified `/usage` command, and you can now create and switch between named custom themes from `/theme`. These changes streamline the UI and reduce command clutter.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.116 Released
highlights.summary: Resuming large sessions (40MB+) is now up to 67% faster, which is a big win if you work on long-running coding sessions. MCP server startup has also been made faster by deferring `resources/templates/list` loading until the first `@`-mention. If you've been frustrated by slow session restores or MCP lag, this update is worth grabbing.

highlights.title: An update on recent Claude Code quality reports
highlights.summary: Anthropic published a public postmortem addressing quality issues that users reported with Claude Code around April 23. Reading this document helps developers understand what went wrong, how Anthropic is responding, and what safeguards are being put in place. If you noticed unexpected behavior recently, this post explains the root cause and the fix timeline.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest addition to the Opus model family, which powers the most capable Claude Code interactions. Developers using Claude Code should be aware of this new model as it may be available as a selectable option and could improve results on complex coding tasks. Check the Anthropic blog for details on capability improvements and pricing.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube video offers a concise 30-minute walkthrough designed to get developers productive with Claude Code quickly. It's ideal for those who want a structured overview without committing to a multi-hour course. Watch it to learn the core workflows, key commands, and best practices that experienced users rely on daily.

highlights.title: Ask HN: How are you using AI code assistants on large messy legacy code bases?
highlights.summary: This Hacker News thread gathers real-world experiences from developers with 20+ years of experience trying to apply AI coding tools like Claude to complex legacy codebases. The discussion surfaces practical strategies for where AI excels (e.g., boilerplate, tests) and where it struggles (e.g., deeply tangled business logic). It's a valuable read for teams evaluating how to roll out Claude Code on existing projects.

highlights.title: FULL Claude Code Tutorial for Beginners in 2026! (Step-By-Step)
highlights.summary: A comprehensive step-by-step video tutorial aimed at complete beginners who want to start using Claude Code from scratch in 2026. It walks through installation, basic commands, and building a simple project using Claude Code integrated with VS Code. This is a great starting point if you're new to AI-assisted coding and want hands-on guidance.

tips_of_the_day: After upgrading to v2.1.119, run `/config` and set your preferred theme and editor mode once — these will now persist automatically to `~/.claude/settings.json` so you never have to reconfigure after a restart.

If you're working on a large legacy codebase with Claude Code, start by asking Claude to generate tests for a single isolated module rather than refactoring broadly — this builds trust in the AI's output while producing immediate, verifiable value.

Use the new `/usage` command (formerly `/cost` and `/stats`) to keep an eye on your token consumption during long coding sessions, especially when working with complex multi-file tasks that can quietly rack up large context windows.
